tree.title: What should I watch tonight?
tree.description: A decision tree to help you pick something to watch.

branch.content: How are you feeling right now?
branch.fork: Want to be on the edge of my seat
Feeling emotional, want a good story

branch.content: Solo or watching with someone?
Full commitment or half-watching?
Seen it before or something new?
Pick something you know makes you laugh. Familiarity is half the appeal. Let yourself sink into it.
A well-reviewed comedy from the last few years that you have been meaning to watch. Check Letterboxd rather than Rotten Tomatoes for the real consensus.
Dialogue-driven or visual comedy?
A sitcom you know well. The Office, Seinfeld, Arrested Development. Familiar and funny, no concentration required.
A sketch show or stand-up you have already seen. Easy to dip in and out of without missing anything important.
Same sense of humour?
Discover something together or a trusted favourite?
Pick something neither of you has seen. Shared discovery of a film you both end up loving is one of the better evenings there is.
Rewatch something you both know is funny. Anticipating the best moments together is its own pleasure.
Mixed ages or similar?
A Pixar or Studio Ghibli film. Works at every level of sophistication. Nobody feels excluded.
A broad crowd-pleaser with physical comedy and a clear story. Accessible without being childish.
What kind of tension?
branch.fork: Horror — I want to be genuinely scared

branch.content: How big is your screen?
Brains or pure spectacle?
A blockbuster built for exactly this. Turn the volume up. Lean in. This is what the screen is for.
A big-budget action film where the set pieces are earned. Mad Max: Fury Road territory. Rare but it exists.
Pure action or story with it?
Tight, kinetic action with clean choreography. John Wick-style works well on a small screen. The action is precise enough not to need scale.
A slower action-thriller where characters earn the set pieces. Much better on a small screen than spectacle-first films.
Patient or need a fast hook?
European or American?
Scandinavian noir or a French thriller. Slow, precise, often very dark. Give it 20 minutes before you judge it.
A deliberate American slow-burner. Fincher territory. Meticulous, cold, and worth the patience.
Feature film or series?
Something under 100 minutes that opens in the middle of the action. Look for a tight runtime and strong first act.
A limited series where the first episode does a lot of work quickly. Better than a film when you want to be hooked and have more time.
What scares you more?
Slow build or tightly paced?
Watch with the lights off and the sound up. The quiet moments are the scariest. Let it breathe. Do not look up anything about it beforehand.
A horror under 90 minutes with a clear setup and a gut-punch ending. Less room for the atmosphere to sag.
Standalone or franchise?
The first film in a horror franchise is almost always the best. Pick the original and stop there.
Watch the first two films back to back and stop before the third. That is usually where the law of diminishing returns kicks in.
Do you want to cry or just feel moved?
Healing cry or a full wallow?
branch.fork: Healing — I want to feel better after

branch.content: Recent loss or just generally low?
A film about grief that ends somewhere like acceptance. Watch it in the dark, alone, with something warm to drink.
A bittersweet film that acknowledges sadness without drowning in it. Should leave you feeling soft and a little more human.
Company or solitude?
Warn your companion in advance. Pick the saddest film you both agree on. Tissues and snacks.
Pick the saddest film you know and commit to it alone. Have tissues ready. You will feel oddly better by the end.
Drama or something uplifting?
Period or contemporary?
A historical drama with great performances and production. Something that transports you out of your current moment completely.
A character study set now. Something that shows you a life you would never otherwise see. The best ones make you feel less alone.
Underdog story or human connection?
You already know they will make it. Watching the journey is the whole point. The ending should feel earned, not cheap.
A quiet film about people finding each other. The kind that leaves you smiling and a little teary in the best possible way.
How much mental energy do you actually have?
branch.fork: A lot — I want a real challenge

branch.content: Fiction or documentary?
Narrative complexity or philosophical ideas?
A film that plays with time, structure, or reality. Something you will want to look up and discuss immediately after watching.
A film that asks large questions through character and story. The kind that quietly changes how you think about something.
Topic?
A documentary that reveals how something works that you had never thought about. The best ones reframe the obvious.
A beautifully made film about the world. The kind that makes you feel small and grateful in equal measure.
A portrait of a creative or a movement. Usually leaves you wanting to make something yourself.
Feature or series?
An intelligent film with a sharp script that does not demand total focus. Good dialogue, clear story, ideas carried through character.
A critically acclaimed series with a self-contained first season. Something you can start without committing to years of viewing.
